nativeRange.habitat: Standing and slow-flowing freshwaters — lowland ponds, ditches, lakes, and slow rivers, typically over fine, organically rich substrates; tolerates calcareous and slightly brackish conditions.

decorPreferences: Fine, organically rich substrate (loam/sand/clay mix or planted-tank soil)
Open background space for long stems
Cool, well-lit setup — does well in unheated or coldwater aquaria

goodWith: Goldfish (in ponds, with caveats — may be uprooted)
avoidWith: Outdoor release of any kind — strictly invasive

summary: A hardy, cold-tolerant pondweed with distinctively serrated, wavy submerged leaves; well-suited to unheated aquaria and ponds, but a serious invasive species outside its native range — never release.
careNotes: INVASIVE-SPECIES WARNING: Potamogeton crispus is one of the most damaging aquatic invaders in North America (established in 40+ U.S. states), South America, and New Zealand, where it outcompetes native submerged vegetation. It MUST NEVER be released into any natural waterbody, and in many jurisdictions sale or possession is restricted or banned (e.g. several New England states, parts of the Upper Midwest). Verify local regulations before purchasing. Disposal should be by drying and trashing — never composting into watercourses. In captivity, this is a forgiving cool-to-temperate plant: distinctive serrated, wavy submerged leaves (25–95 mm long, 5–12 mm wide) on a flattened branching stem that can reach a meter or more in length. It is one of the few aquarium-suitable stem plants that tolerates low light and low temperatures (commonly cited as low as 1–4°C in the wild), which makes it more at home in unheated tanks and ponds than in tropical setups; it tends to decline above ~25°C, going dormant via turions in summer in the wild. Reproduces vegetatively through stem cuttings AND through dormant turions (specialized winter buds) that drop and overwinter — a key trait distinguishing it from most aquarium stem plants. Generally considered beginner-easy: low-to-medium light, no CO2 needed, planted in a nutrient-rich substrate. Growth tends to be fast under good light and can outpace tankmates, so periodic trimming is generally advised. Source notes: Tropica does not catalog this species (originally-assigned Tropica URL was a misroute to Hydrocotyle verticillata), so primary source was swapped to Wikipedia + RHS for taxonomy/morphology/native range, with Glass Aqua and Plantiary providing aquarium-specific husbandry parameters. Sources broadly agreed on cold tolerance, invasiveness, and propagation modes; reported temperature spans (10–24°C aquarium-suitable per Plantiary; survival 1–4°C in wild per USGS) are wider than typical tropical plants and the range here biases toward the aquarium-husbandry window.
breedingNotes: Propagated vegetatively from stem cuttings or by collecting dormant turions (winter buds) that form along the stem and drop to the substrate. Turions overwinter and sprout new plants — distinctive among aquarium stem plants. Flowers (insignificant, reddish, summer) and seeds occur but seeds are rarely viable in aquaria.

primary.name: Wikipedia
primary.notes: Primary source swapped from Tropica to Wikipedia: the assigned Tropica URL (plantdetails/4457/4457) actually serves a Hydrocotyle verticillata page and Tropica does not catalog Potamogeton crispus. Wikipedia provided authoritative taxonomy, morphology, native range, and reproduction (turions).

additional.name: Glass Aqua
additional.notes: Aquarium-trade source confirming low-to-medium lighting, optional CO2, fast growth rate, nutrient-rich substrate preference, and beginner/easy difficulty; also confirms tolerance of low light, low temperatures, and slightly brackish water.

additional.name: Plantiary
additional.notes: Cross-checked aquarium parameters: temperature 10–24°C (50–75°F), pH 6.5–8.5, moderate-to-high light, nutrient-rich substrate, propagation by stem cuttings and turions.

additional.name: Royal Horticultural Society
additional.notes: Confirmed native to GB/Ireland, pH tolerance across acid/neutral/alkaline, H7 hardiness (survives below -20°C), and ultimate spread of 1–1.5 m over 5–10 years.

additional.name: USGS NAS / Wisconsin DNR / Washington Dept of Ecology
additional.notes: Authoritative for invasive-species status in North America: established in 40+ U.S. states, priority management species in the Great Lakes and Midwest; survives at 1–4°C and under ice.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Curly Pondweed is a regulated invasive species in many regions — sale, possession, or transport is restricted or banned in much of North America (multiple U.S. states, several New England jurisdictions) and elsewhere. Verify local regulations before acquiring, and NEVER release into any natural waterway.
